title: Inconsistent API behavior when using unix socket vs the cli tool
body:
With the latest version `3.4.1` on Ubuntu 20.04 here are my scenarios

## Scenario 1

1. `podman system service --time=0 unix:///tmp/podman.socket --log-level=debug`
2. `podman system reset --force --log-level=debug`

Then I try to get a list of all the containers

Request 1 - `curl -iv --unix-socket /tmp/podman.socket http://d/v3.0.0/libpod/containers/json`

Server responds with `500`

```
< HTTP/1.1 500 Internal Server Error
< Api-Version: 1.40
< Libpod-Api-Version: 3.4.1

{"cause":"open /home/istoica/.local/share/containers/storage/libpod/bolt_state.db: no such file or directory","message":"error opening database /home/istoica/.local/share/containers/storage/libpod/bolt_state.db: open /home/istoica/.local/share/containers/storage/libpod/bolt_state.db: no such file or directory","response":500}
```

Immediately after I issue the command again

Request 1 - `curl -iv --unix-socket /tmp/podman.socket http://d/v3.0.0/libpod/containers/json`

It just hangs


## Scenario 2

1. `podman system service --time=0 unix:///tmp/podman.socket --log-level=debug`
2. `podman system reset --force --log-level=debug`

The I try to get a list of all the containers using podman cli

3. `podman container list --format=json` => returns correctly `[]`

I try again to get a list using the http over unix socket
4. `curl -iv --unix-socket /tmp/podman.socket http://d/v3.0.0/libpod/containers/json`

Responds ok
```
< HTTP/1.1 200 OK
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
< Api-Version: 1.40
< Content-Type: text/plain; charset=us-ascii
< Libpod-Api-Version: 3.4.1

< 
[]
```

Although `Content-Type` should be `application/json` or `text/json`

Am I doing/understanding something wrong ?

comments.body:
Looks like the podman database was corrupted or not created correctly and that is why the first API call failed, but calling the podman with the cli reset/fixed the database and that is why it worked with the API call after that. 

I tested this out locally on fedora and the API call worked fine in the first call. @jwhonce @mheon PTAL.

Each podman command will initialize the database on startup. If you start the service before you remove everything including the db with `podman system reset` it will fail because the database no longer exists. The service will only create the db on startup and not for every http query.

Is there a reason why you cannot restart the service after `podman system reset`?

@Luap99 the reasons are much usability, mostly boils down to `It is hard to start/stop the service in a cross platform manner` (I do't know the pid, I dont know if I have access to kill the service, it is different to start/stop linux/mac/windows)

- Can't `podman system reset` itself re-create the database ?
- The service creates the db for every cli command, isn't it consistent to ensure it has it for every http command too ?

In `Scenario 2` I did not need to restart the service, I only needed to call `podman container list --format=json` at least once, the service kept running all this time.

Just some other ideeas

- If it is a `500` and is an expected error, why not a `4XX` one ?
- Do respond with `applications/json` content-type is that is the shape of it

For now, I am not restarting it, but just make sure I at least read once using the cli.

> Can't podman system reset itself re-create the database ?

This command should remove everything in case you want to uninstall podman for example. It should not recreate stuff.

> The service creates the db for every cli command, isn't it consistent to ensure it has it for every http command too ?

This would have a huge performance impact and might not even be possible with the current code architecture.

> In Scenario 2 I did not need to restart the service, I only needed to call podman container list --format=json at least once, the service kept running all this time.

Because every cli command will create the db again.

> It is hard to start/stop the service in a cross platform manner (I do't know the pid, I dont know if I have access to kill the service, it is different to start/stop linux/mac/windows)

Both the `podman system service` and `podman system reset` will only work on linux. Why do you need to run podman system reset at all? If you just want to delete images, containers, etc.. you can use `podman system prune`

> If it is a 500 and is an expected error, why not a 4XX one ?

I would not say that this is expected. 400 codes are for client errors, e.g. the specified container/image/network was not found. If the db does not exists it is a server error.

> Do respond with applications/json content-type is that is the shape of it

Please create a separate bug for this.

@mheon WDYT, can the service recreate the db while running?

Um. We'd have to stop the old Runtime and get a new copy - theoretically possible, but I see the potential for memory leaks (runtime.Shutdown() isn't well tested, I would bet money it isn't closing everything). Also - how do we know that we need to spin up a new Runtime? We could do it every time a request comes in but that would be murderous for performance.

I don't think we can do a DB reset within a given Libpod instance, either. There's too much init logic around the current state of the DB - if the DB disappears from under us we can't just make another, we have to do a full runtime init again.
But then does it make sense to still keep it running after one calls `podman system reset`  ?

Question is, how does the service detect a `podman system reset` and know to restart its Libpod Runtime. We aren't maintaining a constant connection to the DB, so we don't know it was removed from under us until we next try to connect. We could try monitoring events, but that could be unreliable. I don't know if we can reliably catch the "missing database" errors, but if we can, making a fresh Runtime in response could be a good plan.

I have actually done some tampering with a `podman system setup` type feature in one of my most recent PRs @mheon #11958 I think having an explicit command or at least function to initialize a bare bones DB could be helpful for some edge cases. @Luap99 thoughts on that? My current implementation is probably a little far off what we actually want

I don't really think it helps here. We don't really want to do anything more than call `libpod.NewRuntime()` to cause a full re-initialization of the Runtime; the question is when to do it, and how to know we need to.
We should do it when we receive a sighup and then `systemctl  reload podman` would work.

IMO I still do not understand the use case behind this. If you run `podman system reset` you want to delete everything why would you want to keep using the old podman service? I think it would be better if `podman system reset` would just kill this process.

If you only want to delete things use `podman system prune` and not reset.
Makes sense to me.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Would it just make sense to have podman system reset do a 
systemctl try-restart podman.service
or systemctl --user try-restart podman.service
@cdoern would you like to take a look.
@rhatdan sure, I am in this region anyway with my `podman system setup` work

I still do not see why this is needed, `podman system prune` should work just fine.
Restarting the service via systemd will not work when the user manually runs the service as it is the case in the original report.

If I have a long running service waiting for a connection, ( I think we run for 5 minutes) then someone does podman system reset, connections to the service will fail until the service stops.  Stopping the service ourselves fixes that problem.
I agree if a user runs a service themselves, then it is there problem.

SGTM
@vrothberg WDYT?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@vrothberg still need feedback.

That got lost over Christmas, apologies :)

SGTM, we just need to consider systems without systemd as well. The error should probably only be logged on info level.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@cdoern, do you have cycles to tackle the issue?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@iongion @cdoern is this one fixed?
we decided that the behavior we were going to implement is not podman's prerogative to handle, but the users (aka just run a systemctl stop and restart)
